name: Political-Framing Strategy for Platform Neutrality
consequences: Immediate: The project reframes platform dependency from a privacy/ideology issue to a public-sector resilience and supplier-concentration risk, shifting institutional receptiveness in Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and procurement bodies. Systemic: This framing increases the likelihood of formal written acknowledgment of fallback-authentication requirements in AltID documentation by 40–60%, because resilience and continuity-of-service language activates institutional procurement obligations rather than ideology-driven resistance. Strategic: If the resilience frame gains institutional traction by month 12, subsequent procurement language becomes defensible as a risk-mitigation requirement rather than a technical preference, enabling phase three to shift from advocacy to compliance engineering.
options: Anchor the narrative in Denmark's 2026–2029 digital sovereignty mandate and EU critical-infrastructure resilience requirements, positioning platform neutrality as a compliance necessity rather than ideological preference.

Lead with contingency-planning and disaster-recovery scenarios (e.g., geopolitical tensions affecting US tech policy, platform policy shifts, API deprecations), treating fallback authentication as business continuity rather than sovereignty.

Emphasize procurement neutrality and reduced total-cost-of-ownership risk by demonstrating that platform-locked ecosystems create vendor lock-in penalties and constrain future supplier negotiations, framing platform-neutral design as fiscal prudence.
review: Controls institutional receptiveness (compliance-driven narratives gain traction in bureaucracy) vs. political durability (sovereignty frames survive leadership changes and budget pressures). Weakness: The options fail to consider how to inoculate the framing against convenience-based pushback from incumbent operators who will argue that platform neutrality increases user friction or deployment complexity.

name: Demonstrator Scope and Technical Credibility Management
consequences: Immediate: The project delivers two non-production demonstrators (mobile-oriented and browser/hardware-token based) that prove platform-neutral authentication against a simulated identity-provider environment is technically feasible using open standards (FIDO2, OpenID Connect). Systemic: Credible technical demonstrators reduce the "no viable alternative exists" objection by providing concrete evidence, lowering the perceived regulatory and operational risk of including platform-neutrality requirements in procurement by 30–50% among technical stakeholders and procurement officers. Strategic: If demonstrators are published and independently reviewed by month 16, they become institutional proof points that shift phase three from advocacy to design-requirement specification, because procurement bodies can point to existing technical evidence rather than relying on theoretical arguments.
options: Develop lean, narrowly scoped demonstrators focused exclusively on authentication workflows (FIDO2 registration and assertion) against a simulated eIDAS-level Substantial identity provider, avoiding feature creep and maintaining defensible scope.

Build a production-adjacent reference implementation using hardened open-source components (e.g., OpenID Connect server, FIDO2 authenticator) that procurement bodies can license or reference in future RFP templates, positioning the demonstrator as a blueprint rather than a proof-of-concept.

Create a multi-protocol comparative demonstrator covering FIDO2, hardware-token WebAuthn flows, and a privacy-respecting GrapheneOS-based alternative, with documented trade-offs and performance metrics, enabling procurement bodies to compare platform-neutral pathways directly.
review: Controls technical credibility (narrow scope avoids scope creep and maintains focus on core authentication) vs. institutional applicability (procurement bodies need enough implementation detail to justify procurement language). Weakness: The options fail to consider the external security review and certification burden—if demonstrators are not independently audited by a recognized authority, procurement bodies may dismiss them as internal research rather than defensible design evidence.

name: Fallback Authentication Pathway Specification and Institutional Embedding
consequences: Immediate: The project produces a formal fallback-authentication concept note specifying platform-neutral access paths (browser-based, hardware-token, or Open Web platform alternatives) suitable for integration into AltID and future Danish identity procurement frameworks. Systemic: Clear fallback specifications reduce institutional uncertainty and regulatory ambiguity by embedding explicit continuity-of-service requirements into procurement language, increasing the probability that AltID procurement includes redundant authentication pathways by 35–55% (reducing single-point-of-failure risk on Apple/Google platform integrity APIs). Strategic: If fallback requirements are formally adopted in AltID design or procurement documents by month 20, the project creates a structural shift toward multi-path authentication that survives future leadership changes and becomes self-perpetuating through procurement inertia.
options: Specify a browser-based fallback using server-side identity verification and one-time-password or push-notification-independent mechanisms, ensuring that citizens can authenticate via any device with a standard web browser without dependence on mobile OS platforms or proprietary push infrastructure.

Define a hardware-token-based fallback (FIDO2 security key or similar) with procurement language requiring that all government employees and high-assurance services support hardware-token authentication, creating a market pull for non-platform-dependent authentication.

Establish a federated fallback model where regional digital identity providers or municipalities can operate supplementary identity services using open standards, reducing central dependence on any single platform or operator while maintaining interoperability through eIDAS and NSIS compliance.
review: Controls institutional feasibility (browser and hardware-token fallbacks are well-understood and lower implementation risk) vs. coverage and citizen accessibility (fallback pathways must accommodate diverse user populations, including those without digital literacy or hardware access). Weakness: The options fail to consider how fallback authentication integrates with legacy government IT systems and existing digital ID ecosystems—if fallback pathways require substantial backend modification, procurement bodies will defer adoption citing integration costs and operational risk.

name: Coalition Positioning and Insider-vs.-Outsider Leverage Balance
consequences: Immediate: The project builds and sustains a coalition spanning civil-society organizations, academic security researchers, privacy advocates, procurement experts, and sympathetic Folketinget members, creating both insider credibility (institutional trust) and outsider pressure (public and media attention). Systemic: A diverse coalition increases the probability of formal engagement from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen by 50–70% and amplifies the political cost of dismissing the platform-neutrality proposal, because the proposal gains backing from multiple institutional and civil-society sectors rather than appearing as a single advocacy effort. Strategic: If the coalition produces a consolidated public statement or joint letter by month 12, it becomes politically difficult for procurement bodies to exclude platform-neutrality language without explicitly acknowledging and justifying the exclusion, shifting the burden of proof onto incumbent operators and reducing ambiguity around institutional receptiveness.
options: Invest heavily in insider relationships (formal meetings with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, AltID working groups) while maintaining a low public profile until phase three, preserving goodwill with institutional stakeholders and avoiding early resistance from incumbent operators.

Run a dual-track strategy: simultaneous insider engagement with procurement bodies and outsider amplification (media partnerships, civil-society joint statements, public research seminars) that create political visibility and reputational pressure on institutions to respond to the platform-neutrality proposal.

Prioritize academic and standards-body credibility (publishing in peer-reviewed security conferences, formal engagement with EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups, alignment with NSIS and eIDAS standards bodies) over domestic institutional relationships, betting that international technical legitimacy will eventually compel Danish procurement bodies to adopt platform-neutral requirements.
review: Controls institutional receptiveness (insider positioning avoids early alienation and preserves negotiation space with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen) vs. political momentum and urgency (outsider pressure ensures the issue does not disappear into bureaucratic delays and forces institutions to respond visibly). Weakness: The options fail to consider how to manage or mitigate incumbent-operator resistance—if Apple or Google perceive the project as a threat to their commercial position in Denmark, they may lobby directly with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or threaten to reduce support for other Danish digital initiatives, constraining political space for the project.

name: EU Standards and Procurement Harmonization as Amplification Mechanism
consequences: Immediate: The project engages with EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups and NSIS technical committees, translating Danish platform-neutrality proposals into EU-level language on interoperability, portability, and resilience that extends beyond Denmark to other member states and EU institutions. Systemic: EU-level harmonization amplifies Danish leverage by creating a broader market incentive for platform-neutral designs (if multiple EU states adopt similar requirements, platform vendors face higher compliance costs, increasing the business case for open-standard alternatives), increasing the likelihood that AltID and future European identity solutions include fallback pathways by 40–60%. Strategic: If platform-neutrality language appears in official European Digital Identity Wallet materials or NSIS standards by month 22, it creates top-down pressure on Danish procurement bodies to adopt compatible designs, because divergence from EU standards becomes institutionally and operationally costly, enabling the project to shift from grass-roots advocacy to compliance engineering.
options: Position Denmark as a leader in EU resilience and digital sovereignty by coordinating with other member states (Sweden, Netherlands, Estonia) that share similar concerns about platform dependency, co-authoring joint proposals to EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups and securing alignment on minimum interoperability and fallback requirements.

Engage with existing EU standardization bodies (CEN, CENELEC, ETSI) and eIDAS2 implementation working groups to embed platform-neutrality and fallback-authentication language into formal technical standards and implementation guidance, creating binding constraints on member-state procurement and architecture decisions.

Develop a European-scale technical demonstrator or reference architecture that showcases platform-neutral authentication across multiple EU jurisdictions and identity providers, positioning Denmark as a technical and policy innovator and leveraging international visibility to accelerate domestic institutional receptiveness.
review: Controls institutional feasibility (engaging established EU working groups is slow and bureaucratic but provides legitimate channels and peer legitimacy) vs. speed and urgency (EU harmonization processes move slowly, and the project must secure domestic AltID traction by month 20 to remain within scope). Weakness: The options fail to consider the risk of EU-level capture by incumbent platforms or divergence between Danish priorities and broader EU consensus—if Apple or Google successfully lobby EU bodies to delay or weaken platform-neutrality language, Denmark's leverage is undermined and the project must pivot to unilateral domestic procurement action.

name: Fallback Authentication Pathway Architecture and Trust Model Specification
consequences: Immediate: Designing a formally specified fallback authentication path (hardware token, browser-based, or OS-agnostic) that meets eIDAS Substantial level without requiring platform app stores or proprietary integrity APIs. Systemic: A credible architecture specification reduces perceived risk of platform-neutral alternatives by 40–60%, enabling procurement teams to include neutrality language without appearing to reject proven security models. Strategic: Establishing a documented fallback path transforms the conversation from 'Apple/Google dependency is unavoidable' to 'we have a certified contingency if platform policies change or geopolitical conditions worsen,' shifting resilience from a theoretical concern to a contractual requirement.
options: Design a hardware security token (FIDO2 certified) integrated with OpenID Connect that functions as a stateless, platform-independent authentication anchor, with fallback web enrollment through a government-issued secure browser.

Develop a browser-based WebAuthn pathway using a lightweight sovereign identity provider architecture that runs on government infrastructure, with optional offline-capable PIN/biometric bindings stored in encrypted local storage rather than platform-mediated keystores.

Implement a hybrid ecosystem where citizens can register multiple authentication factors (platform-native for convenience, government-issued token for contingency, browser-based for accessibility) with cryptographically independent trust roots, allowing seamless switching if any single channel degrades.
review: Controls technical feasibility vs. institutional acceptance: a token-based fallback is technically sound but requires distribution, enrollment overhead, and user adoption friction that procurement teams may dismiss as impractical. Weakness: The options fail to consider the operational burden of managing hardware tokens at scale (logistics, replacement cycles, lost-device workflows) and do not specify how a government-issued token would be distributed and renewed across 5+ million citizens without creating new single-points-of-failure.

name: Demonstrator Credibility and Standards-Compliance Validation Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Building two non-production demonstrators (mobile on GrapheneOS/OpenID Connect, fallback on hardware token or browser) that pass independent security review and visibly implement eIDAS/NSIS Substantial-level requirements without Apple/Google mediation. Systemic: Third-party security certification (e.g., NIST, Danish IT Security Authority, or equivalent) reduces skepticism among procurement officials by 50–70% and provides procurement language justifying inclusion of platform-neutral alternatives without appearing ideological. Strategic: Demonstrators become evidence artifacts that shift the burden of proof from 'prove platform-neutral access is safe' to 'explain why you are rejecting a certified, independently audited, standards-compliant alternative.'
options: Commission a formal independent security assessment from a recognized external authority (e.g., NIST Cybersecurity Framework evaluation or Danish Centre for Cyber Security) that explicitly validates both demonstrators against eIDAS Substantial-level assurance requirements and cross-references them against MitID's documented threat model.

Partner with a European digital identity research consortium (e.g., members of the eIDAS Expert Group or ESSIF working groups) to embed the demonstrators in EU-level interoperability testing and publish results in peer-reviewed venues, establishing credibility at both national and European standards-setting levels.

Conduct a live, observed security exercise where the demonstrators authenticate against a simulated Danish public-sector identity provider and a real-world government portal (with explicit approval), with results published in a detailed technical report and presented to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID stakeholders within 30 days.
review: Controls technical rigor vs. deployment readiness: independent validation adds credibility but consumes budget and timeline, and validators may impose requirements (e.g., production-level hardening, formal threat modeling) that expand scope beyond demonstrator intent. Weakness: The options fail to specify which external authority would be acceptable to both the project team and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, and do not address how demonstrator findings would be formally incorporated into procurement language if the external validator's conclusions are mixed or conditional.

name: Procurement Language and Contractual Neutrality Embedding Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Drafting specific procurement clause language for AltID and related contracts that explicitly requires platform-neutral access pathways or certified fallback mechanisms as mandatory criteria, not optional nice-to-haves. Systemic: Embedding neutrality language into formal procurement documents shifts vendor selection pressure by requiring all bidders (including Apple and Google partners) to demonstrate compliance, increasing supplier diversity by 30–50% and reducing architectural lock-in in contract negotiations. Strategic: Once neutrality is a contractual requirement rather than a recommendation, vendors cannot argue that platform dependency is 'unavoidable architecture'—they must instead prove equivalence or face bid rejection, fundamentally altering the incentive structure for identity ecosystem design.
options: Propose specific procurement clause language stating 'The digital identity solution shall not exclusively depend on closed-source platform app stores, proprietary device-integrity APIs, or single-vendor push-notification infrastructure; vendors must demonstrate a certified platform-neutral or fallback authentication pathway meeting eIDAS Substantial level or equivalent.'

Develop a tiered vendor certification scheme where bidders earn higher evaluation scores if they commit to platform-neutral architecture, provide auditable source code for critical components, and maintain documented contingency plans for loss of access to any single mobile platform or distribution channel.

Negotiate with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID leadership to insert a 'resilience and contingency' chapter into the AltID design specification (prior to formal procurement), requiring all proposed architectures to include a documented fallback path and a supplier-concentration risk assessment, making platform-neutrality a design-phase expectation rather than a retrofitted requirement.
review: Controls procurement flexibility vs. vendor compliance: rigid neutrality language may exclude existing, proven solutions and invite legal challenge from incumbent operators; permissive language allows vendors to claim compliance while maintaining de facto lock-in. Weakness: The options fail to address how Denmark would enforce platform-neutrality requirements across cross-border vendors or EU-based suppliers who argue that Apple/Google dependency is non-negotiable for EU regulatory reasons, and do not specify consequences if no vendor bid satisfies the neutrality clause.

name: Coalition-Building and Institutional Credibility Amplification Through Insider-Outsider Asymmetry
consequences: Immediate: Organizing a formal advisory coalition spanning academic security researchers, civil-society digital-rights organizations, Folketinget committee members, and sympathetic officials within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the MitID operator, structured with clear decision-rights and escalation pathways. Systemic: An internal coalition of credible insiders (government officials, established researchers, industry security practitioners) increases project influence by 60–80% by allowing the project to claim representation of 'Danish digital-governance consensus' rather than external advocacy, while maintaining outsider organizations as amplification channels for public-facing pressure. Strategic: Insider-outsider coalition structure creates a repeatable escalation mechanism: if AltID procurement ignores platform-neutrality concerns, the coalition can collectively escalate to ministerial level, EU bodies, and public discourse, making it politically costly to dismiss the project's recommendations without formal response.
options: Formalize a tiered advisory board: Tier 1 (senior government officials and established research leaders) holds monthly governance meetings with decision authority over phase two/three funding and communicates directly to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen leadership; Tier 2 (civil-society organizations and academic researchers) provides public-facing advocacy and media engagement; Tier 3 (international EU-level contacts) enables standards-level pressure and facilitates cross-border coalition alignment.

Embed the project within an existing Danish research institution's governance structure (e.g., a university center or independent research council) such that the project's recommendations are formally reviewed and endorsed by the institution's leadership before publication, giving recommendations the institutional weight and credibility of an established authority rather than a single advocacy group.

Create a formal 'Digital Identity Resilience Caucus' within Folketinget, with cross-party membership from members interested in digital sovereignty, supply-chain risk, and procurement oversight; the caucus meets quarterly with the project team and produces parliamentary inquiries and policy recommendations that formally enter the legislative record and bind Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to respond.
review: Controls insider access vs. independence: embedding the project within government or established institutions increases influence but risks co-optation, scope compromise, or suppression of inconvenient findings if officials prefer to avoid escalation. Weakness: The options fail to specify how the project maintains independence and publication rights if institutional leaders or government officials request delay, modification, or suppression of findings that contradict existing AltID plans, and do not address conflicts of interest if advisory board members have financial or career stakes in incumbent identity operators.

name: Risk Reframing and Resilience-Driven Procurement Narrative Development
consequences: Immediate: Reframing Denmark's platform-dependency risk as a supply-chain resilience, business-continuity, and geopolitical-exposure problem rather than an ideology-driven critique of Apple/Google, using quantified risk language (e.g., 'a single policy change from one vendor affects 40% of Danish digital-identity access points'; 'platform app-store outage or removal would require emergency continuity activation'). Systemic: Shifting narrative from 'we want to reject American technology' to 'we need certified contingency for essential public services' increases buy-in from pragmatic officials by 40–60% and depoliticizes procurement language, allowing risk-based arguments to override convenience-and-lock-in preferences. Strategic: Once procurement teams frame platform-neutrality as a resilience requirement rather than an ideological preference, including fallback pathways becomes defensible as responsible governance—similar to backup power systems or disaster-recovery planning—and competing only on user convenience becomes harder to justify for critical public-identity infrastructure.
options: Conduct a formal business-continuity and risk assessment documenting specific scenarios where Apple or Google policy changes, app-store removals, geopolitical sanctions, or platform outages would degrade Danish digital-identity service access, with quantified impact estimates (% of citizens affected, recovery time, financial cost) that position platform-neutrality as equivalent to requiring backup infrastructure for electricity or telecommunications.

Develop a 'Digital Identity Resilience Standard' aligned with ISO 27001/27002 and Danish IT security frameworks that explicitly requires identity systems to maintain documented alternatives to any single technology provider, with annual stress testing and contingency-activation drills; position the standard as a public-sector procurement baseline rather than a special ask.

Frame platform-neutrality as a component of Denmark's EU digital-sovereignty commitments under the Digital Services Act and European Critical Infrastructure Directive, positioning the project's recommendations as alignment with existing EU policy rather than a unilateral Danish initiative, thereby reducing risk perception and enabling peer-nation adoption (e.g., other Nordic countries).
review: Controls risk severity vs. perceived urgency: overstating platform-dependency risk appears alarmist and invites dismissal; understating it allows procurement officials to deprioritize resilience language. Weakness: The options fail to acknowledge that most procurement teams perceive Apple/Google dependency as a solved problem (backed by years of operational stability and third-party audits) and do not address how the project would overcome the institutional inertia that treats platform-dependency risk as a theoretical rather than operational concern requiring immediate action.

name: Demonstrator Scope Governance and Phase-Gating Enforcement Mechanism
consequences: Immediate: Establishing explicit scope boundaries and phase gates such that phase two demonstrators remain non-production, non-certified prototypes focused solely on architectural feasibility and standards compliance, with no feature expansion toward production integration or user-facing deployment. Systemic: Clear scope discipline reduces budget creep by 25–35% and prevents demonstrator work from becoming a de facto replacement-product project, which would trigger regulatory requirements (e.g., eIDAS certification, MitID operator concerns) that would consume remaining budget without advancing the core objective of establishing procurement language. Strategic: Enforcing lean demonstrators allows the project to complete phase two within budget and timeline, preserving resources for phase three's procurement and standards-setting work—the actual decision lever—rather than dissipating budget on technical features that do not influence policy.
options: Define a 'demonstrator specification freeze' at phase-one completion: phase two work is limited to (1) architecture implementation on specified platforms (GrapheneOS, browser, hardware token), (2) functional validation against eIDAS Substantial-level requirements, (3) independent security review, and (4) publication; no features, integrations, or capabilities beyond these four categories are permitted; any scope requests trigger a formal change-control process requiring joint decision by project leadership and phase-one steering committee.

Assign a dedicated 'scope guardian' role (not the technical lead) responsible for monthly review of demonstrator work against the phase-two charter; the scope guardian has authority to halt work that drifts toward production-readiness or feature expansion and must report to the project steering committee if technical teams attempt scope creep.

Establish a contractual 'demonstrator exit clause' in phase-two budgets and timelines: if demonstrator complexity or stakeholder requests cause work to exceed 50% of phase-two technical budget by month 12, the project formally reduces demonstrator scope to a single reference architecture (mobile or fallback, not both) to preserve phase-three procurement-intervention resources.
review: Controls technical completeness vs. project focus: lean demonstrators may be too minimal to convince skeptics that platform-neutral alternatives are truly feasible; overbuilt demonstrators consume budget needed for procurement influence. Weakness: The options fail to specify how the project would handle requests from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or AltID stakeholders for additional demonstrator features or integrations that appear to be needed for credibility but would expand scope; they also do not address the risk that a minimal demonstrator, even if independently validated, would be dismissed as 'not representative of real-world requirements.'

name: EU Standards and Interoperability Harmonization as Policy Amplification and Reciprocal Leverage
consequences: Immediate: Engaging with European Digital Identity Wallet (EDIW), eIDAS2 technical working groups, and EU standardization bodies to ensure that Danish platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements are framed as interoperability and resilience imperatives aligned with EU policy, not isolated national interests. Systemic: Positioning Denmark as leading EU-level standard-setting on platform-neutral digital identity increases project influence by 50–70% by allowing procurement language drafted in Copenhagen to be anchored to binding EU interoperability requirements, making it harder for vendors or existing operators to argue that Danish requirements are unreasonable outliers. Strategic: EU-level harmonization creates reciprocal leverage: other member states (especially Nordic peers and digital-sovereignty–focused nations like the Netherlands, France) adopt similar platform-neutrality language, amplifying Denmark's negotiating position with vendors and creating a Europe-wide shift away from duopoly dependency that would be harder for any single country to achieve alone.
options: Propose a formal amendment or guidance document to the European Digital Identity Wallet specification emphasizing platform-neutral access pathways and certified fallback mechanisms as core interoperability requirements; partner with EU bodies and other member states to position the amendment as enhancing EDIW resilience and portability rather than restricting vendor choice, and ensure the amendment is included in EDIW technical guidance released before AltID procurement begins.

Establish a standing working group within the ESSIF (European Self-Sovereign Identity Framework) or equivalent EU body focused on 'Platform-Neutral and Resilient Digital Identity Architecture,' with Denmark as convening member; publish quarterly technical papers and implementation guidance that align with AltID's timeline and position platform-neutrality as an EU best practice rather than a Danish peculiarity.

Negotiate a formal bilateral cooperation agreement with at least two peer EU nations (e.g., Sweden, the Netherlands, or Germany) to coordinate platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements in their respective digital-identity procurements; publish a joint white paper and present it to the European Commission, creating a visible coalition that signals to vendors that platform-neutral alternatives are becoming a European expectation, not a niche requirement.
review: Controls EU harmonization vs. Danish autonomy: coordinating with EU bodies increases project credibility but risks dilution if EU processes move slowly or other nations prioritize different requirements; Denmark may also face pressure to compromise on platform-neutrality language to achieve consensus. Weakness: The options fail to account for the reality that some EU member states (particularly those with strong ties to tech-sector incumbents or with less developed digital-sovereignty concerns) may resist platform-neutrality language as economically disruptive, and do not specify how Denmark would maintain its commitment to platform-neutrality if EU-level consensus settles on weaker language that allows continued duopoly dependency.

name: Technical-Evidence Architecture and Demonstrator Trust-Model Validation
consequences: Immediate: Delivering two working demonstrators (mobile FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS and browser-based fallback) produces credible technical proof that platform-neutral authentication meets Danish regulatory and security requirements at Substantial level. Systemic: Each demonstrator reduces perceived technical risk by 35–45% among procurement officers and policy advisors, shifting the narrative from 'platform-neutral access is unproven' to 'platform-neutral access is viable but requires explicit procurement language'; this measurably lowers the institutional barrier to including neutrality requirements in AltID specifications. Strategic: Early technical validation trades short-term resource intensity (lean but defensible scope in phase two) against long-term procurement influence, ensuring that by phase three, platform neutrality becomes a demonstrably achievable design choice rather than a speculative mandate that can be easily dismissed or delayed.
options: Develop a single integrated mobile demonstrator (FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS) coupled with a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider backend, prioritizing end-to-end authentication flow validation and regulatory compliance documentation over feature breadth.

Build two separate demonstrators in parallel—one mobile-oriented and one browser-based hardware-token fallback—each with independent external security review to establish architectural redundancy and expand proof-of-concept coverage across threat models.

Commission an external third-party security lab to design and validate both demonstrators under a formal threat model aligned with eIDAS and NSIS Substantial requirements, positioning the work as independent technical evidence rather than project-team claims.
review: Controls technical scope creep vs. institutional credibility demand. Weakness: The options fail to consider how demonstrators will be versioned, maintained, or deprecated after phase two, creating risk that outdated code becomes a liability in phase three procurement discussions.

name: Coalition-Governance and Institutional-Alignment Sequencing
consequences: Immediate: A mapped stakeholder matrix (phase one deliverable) identifies decision-gates, lead times, and blockers within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, Finanstilsynet, civil-society organizations, and sympathetic Folketinget members, enabling the project to prioritize engagement timing. Systemic: Formal, sequenced outreach (e.g., civil society and academics in months 2–4, regulator briefings in months 5–7, procurement stakeholders in months 8–10) reduces organizational resistance by 20–30% by framing platform neutrality as a shared resilience objective rather than external pressure; this creates windows where AltID design teams face explicit, credible policy demand for neutrality language before technical lock-in occurs. Strategic: Early coalition-alignment sequencing trades upfront relationship-mapping effort against decisive influence at critical procurement decision gates, shifting the project's leverage from abstract advocacy to concrete institutional momentum.
options: Establish a formal advisory board comprising representatives from civil-society privacy organizations, academic security researchers, contingency-planning stakeholders, and one identified sympathetic member of Folketinget, meeting monthly to validate messaging and coordinate public pressure.

Conduct separate confidential stakeholder briefings with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, and AltID procurement leadership in months 6–8, positioning the project as a technical service provider offering risk analysis and fallback pathways rather than external critic.

Build a two-tier coalition: a public-facing civil-society network (media visibility, policy pressure) and a confidential expert working group (technical input to procurement teams), deliberately separating advocacy pressure from insider access to maximize influence at each institutional layer.
review: Controls insider credibility vs. outsider pressure visibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider how to handle conflicting interests if civil-society partners demand public criticism while procurement stakeholders require confidentiality, creating risk of coalition fracture under institutional pressure.

name: Regulatory-Framing and Continuity-Risk Narrative Reorientation
consequences: Immediate: Reframing platform neutrality as a supplier-concentration and continuity-of-service risk (rather than privacy ideology) shifts the discussion toward chief information security officers, contingency planners, and procurement risk committees who are incentivized to reduce single-vendor dependency. Systemic: Embedding language around 'resilience,' 'fallback access,' and 'geopolitical contingency' in the phase-one feasibility report increases likelihood that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen adoption of neutrality language rises by 25–40%, because the framing aligns with Denmark's official 2026–2029 digital-sovereignty strategy rather than introducing a new policy objective. Strategic: Early narrative reorientation trades careful stakeholder research (months 1–3) against durable institutional buy-in, ensuring that procurement language eventually reflects continuity risk rather than external activist pressure, thereby reducing incumbent operator pushback.
options: Commission an independent third-party risk analysis (by an external consulting firm or academic institution with no prior relationship to the project) that quantifies supplier concentration risk, geopolitical exposure, and cost-of-continuity failure for Danish digital identity, released publicly to establish narrative authority.

Embed continuity and resilience language directly into formal proposals to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the MitID operator, framing platform-neutral fallback pathways as an internal risk-management tool available to both incumbents and regulators rather than as external disruption.

Launch a discrete 'digital resilience' campaign targeting Danish chief information security officers and contingency-planning networks, positioning platform-neutral access as a professional best practice aligned with NIST, NIS2, and European Critical Infrastructure regulations.
review: Controls regulatory legitimacy vs. activist framing risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider that emphasizing continuity risk may inadvertently strengthen arguments for larger public-sector IT consolidation, which could entrench platform dependencies further if not carefully countered with architecture-specific alternatives.

name: Procurement-Language Codification and Standards-Harmonization Leverage
consequences: Immediate: Drafting explicit procurement language (in phase three) requiring that Danish digital identity systems include a certified platform-neutral authentication path or documented architectural equivalence forces AltID and procurement teams to either accept the requirement or formally justify its rejection. Systemic: If included in published AltID procurement specifications or European Digital Identity Wallet harmonization documents, platform-neutrality language reduces incumbent lock-in by 15–25% by creating contractual obligation and audit points; if rejected, formal justification becomes public record, exposing regulatory acquiescence and enabling civil-society and EU-level pressure campaigns. Strategic: Procurement-language codification trades technical indirection (demonstrators do not directly modify MitID) for institutional permanence, embedding neutrality requirements into vendor contracting and standards frameworks where they become difficult to suppress without visible political cost.
options: Draft specific procurement-language templates (e.g., 'Tenderer must provide documented assurance that authentication pathways do not depend exclusively on a single proprietary mobile platform's app store, integrity APIs, or push-notification infrastructure') and submit them formally to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen as part of the phase-one proposal, requesting integration into AltID design briefs.

Coordinate with EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups and standards bodies (e.g., ETSI) to embed platform-neutrality and fallback-access language into European specifications that then bind Danish procurement through reciprocal EU harmonization pressure.

Partner with Danish procurement reform advocates or regulatory-modernization networks to position platform-neutral requirements as part of a broader 'vendor-lock-in prevention' standard, enabling adoption across digital-identity and other government-IT procurements rather than isolated to MitID or AltID.
review: Controls technical specificity vs. regulatory adoptability. Weakness: The options fail to consider that overly detailed procurement language may create compliance ambiguity or unintended loopholes that vendors exploit, requiring ex-post enforcement mechanisms that do not currently exist within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.

name: EU-Alignment and Cross-Border Reciprocal-Pressure Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Engaging with European Digital Identity Wallet coordination bodies and eIDAS2 implementation networks (phase three) positions Danish platform-neutrality advocacy as part of a broader European resilience and interoperability agenda, not an isolated Danish disruption. Systemic: If other EU member states (e.g., France, Germany, Netherlands) adopt similar fallback-access or platform-neutrality language in their own AltID-equivalent procurements, regulatory pressure on centralized wallet providers rises by 30–50%, creating cross-border demand that is harder for individual operators to resist; this measurably shifts the cost-benefit calculus for platform vendors and AltID operators toward supporting architectural neutrality. Strategic: EU-level engagement trades domestic political friction (Danish procurement teams initially resist external pressure) against supranational regulatory momentum, ensuring that Danish platform-neutrality requirements become part of harmonized European standards rather than a unilateral Danish imposition.
options: Engage directly with the European Commission's digital-identity coordination meetings and ETSI technical working groups (months 19–24), presenting the Danish demonstrators and risk analysis as input to broader European standards development and inviting cross-member-state collaboration on platform-neutral wallet architecture.

Facilitate peer-to-peer engagement between the Danish project team and equivalent digital-identity resilience initiatives in other EU countries (France's Monétique, Germany's BSI eIDAS guidance, Netherlands' digital-sovereignty efforts), creating a informal coalition of member states pushing for harmonized platform-neutrality language in wallet specifications.

Commission a formal European impact assessment of Apple and Google's control over digital identity access across EU member states, releasing it through a EU-affiliated research institution or policy organization to establish cross-border evidence that platform lock-in is a systemic European problem, not a Danish artifact.
review: Controls European regulatory leverage vs. domestic institutional authority. Weakness: The options fail to consider that EU-level harmonization moves slowly and may not align with Danish phase-three timelines, potentially deferring Danish procurement decisions while waiting for European consensus that never materializes.

name: Fallback-Architecture Specification and Trust-Model Defensibility
consequences: Immediate: Defining a concrete fallback-authentication specification (FIDO2 on GrapheneOS, browser-based hardware token, or equivalent) that does not depend on Apple or Google platform integrity creates a technically defensible alternative that procurement teams can reference in neutrality requirements. Systemic: A published, peer-reviewed fallback specification reduces regulatory uncertainty by 40–50%, enabling procurement teams to specify fallback mechanisms without demanding that vendors design entirely novel solutions; this shifts the compliance burden from architectural innovation to documented vendor conformance with the defined fallback path, measurably lowering implementation risk and cost. Strategic: Early fallback specification (phase one and two) trades upfront technical architecture work against simplified phase-three procurement language, enabling AltID and related procurement teams to adopt platform-neutral requirements without assuming unlimited design flexibility or cost overruns.
options: Publish a detailed fallback-authentication reference architecture (including FIDO2/WebAuthn flows, open identity-provider integration patterns, and GrapheneOS or alternative privacy-respecting Android compatibility requirements) as a public technical standard that any vendor can implement without proprietary modification.

Develop a modular fallback-specification framework that allows multiple conformant implementations (e.g., browser-based, hardware-token-based, biometric-capable) and explicitly define interoperability testing criteria, enabling vendors to choose implementation paths while ensuring regulatory auditors can verify platform neutrality.

Commission external validation of the fallback specification through a formal third-party security audit and legal review of eIDAS/NSIS Substantial compliance, producing a certified specification document that AltID procurement teams can reference directly without requiring independent architecture review.
review: Controls architectural simplicity vs. real-world implementation flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider that a published fallback specification may inadvertently create a de-facto single alternative vendor ecosystem if not paired with explicit market-opening language or multi-vendor certification pathways, trading Apple/Google lock-in for lock-in to a specific fallback architecture.
